title: Wood Christmas Tree Decor
summary: Small, medium, and large stylized wood trees from a few feet of scrap 1x boards. They nest together for flat storage the other eleven months.
description:
These little wood trees are pure scrap-bin decor — three feet of any 1x board makes the small one, and you can use 1x2, 1x3, 1x4, or anything wider depending on how chunky you want it. The whole set is nothing but miter cuts and glue.

The geometry is simpler than the cut list makes it look. Every tree is a stack of angled branch rows over a trunk and base, and the two angles never change: the top point is 40° off square, and every other cut is 25° off square. Set your saw, cut all the 25° pieces, then reset for the two 40° top cuts. Rotating the saw to the angle and cutting the boards flat is all a standard miter saw needs; a compound saw lets you bevel if you want a faceted look.

Because the sizes share the same angles, you can invent your own sizes by starting at the top and working down. And they're designed to nest — the smaller tree sits inside the profile of the larger one — so the whole set stores flat in a drawer.

tools.note: Every cut is a 25° or 40° miter. A compound saw lets you add bevels for a faceted look.
Glue plus a brad or two per joint holds the thin branch pieces.

materials.name: 1x board (1x2, 1x3, 1x4 or wider), 3 ft
materials.note: Small tree. Use 4 ft for the medium, 6 ft for the large. Wider stock = chunkier tree.

materials.name: Paint or stain
materials.note: Optional. Raw wood, white-washed, or painted all read well.

cutList.note: Long point to long point; one end 40°, other end 25° off square.
Long point to long point; one end 40°, other end 25°.
Long-to-short point; ends parallel, both 25° off square.
Long point to long point; ends NOT parallel, both 25°.

steps.title: Cut the tree top
steps.body:
Start with the two top pieces. The top point is cut at 40° off square and the bottom of each at 25° off square. Rotate the miter saw to each angle and cut the boards flat; a compound saw lets you bevel instead for a faceted top.

To make your own sizes, start here at the top and work down — the angles stay the same, only the lengths grow.

steps.title: Add the branch rows
steps.body: Measure and mark down 1/2" from the top piece and glue-and-brad-nail on the first row of branches. Work down the tree adding each row the same way, keeping the outer points aligned.

steps.title: Add the base and trunk
steps.body: Attach the base to the bottom of the branch stack, then add the short trunk piece between the base and the lowest branch row. A brad and glue at each joint is plenty for decor weight.

steps.title: Finish, and repeat for the other sizes
steps.body: Finish however you like — raw, white-washed, or painted. Build the medium and large trees the same way with their longer cut lists. Since they share the angles and nest together, the trio stores flat when the season's over.

images.alt: A set of three stylized wood Christmas trees in graduated sizes
